bio.bio_blurb: Lawyer; J.P. Morgan's son-in-law and biographer

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote the authorized life of Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: A New York lawyer, Satterlee married J. Pierpont Morgan's daughter and later wrote the authorized biography J. Pierpont Morgan: An Intimate Portrait. His wife Louisa read proofs aloud to spare the failing eyesight of contributors and deciphered Morgan's difficult hand.

narrative_blocks.header: He served as assistant secretary of the Navy
narrative_blocks.body: A New York lawyer, Satterlee served as Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Theodore Roosevelt in 1908 and 1909. He took an early interest in building an American naval reserve.

narrative_blocks.header: He restored a colonial Maryland plantation
narrative_blocks.body: A descendant of the Livingston family, Satterlee bought and restored Sotterley, an eighteenth-century tobacco estate on the Patuxent River, in 1910. The property became the family's country seat for decades.

children.n: Doctor Mabel Morgan Satterlee Satterlee Ingalls

bio.bio_blurb: Lawyer; J.P. Morgan's son-in-law and biographer

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote the authorized life of Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: A New York lawyer, Satterlee married J. Pierpont Morgan's daughter and later wrote the authorized biography J. Pierpont Morgan: An Intimate Portrait. His wife Louisa read proofs aloud to spare the failing eyesight of contributors and deciphered Morgan's difficult hand.

narrative_blocks.header: He served as assistant secretary of the Navy
narrative_blocks.body: A New York lawyer, Satterlee served as Assistant Secretary of the Navy under Theodore Roosevelt in 1908 and 1909. He took an early interest in building an American naval reserve.

narrative_blocks.header: He restored a colonial Maryland plantation
narrative_blocks.body: A descendant of the Livingston family, Satterlee bought and restored Sotterley, an eighteenth-century tobacco estate on the Patuxent River, in 1910. The property became the family's country seat for decades.

notable.notable_blurb: J.P. Morgan's daughter; International Garden Club president

narrative_blocks.body: Louisa was the eldest daughter of J. Pierpont Morgan. She and her husband Herbert Satterlee owned Dover House in Roehampton, London — a storied estate formerly associated with Napoleon III's court.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband served in the Navy Department
narrative_blocks.body: Louisa married Herbert Livingston Satterlee, a Robert Livingston descendant who was assistant secretary of the navy under Theodore Roosevelt. In 1910 the couple bought Maryland's Sotterley Plantation, now a National Historic Landmark.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter was Morgan's last grandchild
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter, Dr. Mabel Satterlee Ingalls, took a Columbia doctorate in bacteriology and became the last surviving grandchild of J. Pierpont Morgan. She helped found a Goodwill Industries branch and served the family's Pierpont Morgan Library.

narrative_blocks.header: She quietly carried the Morgan name
narrative_blocks.body: A daughter of J. Pierpont Morgan, Louisa shunned publicity while leading wartime relief drives and serving as president of the International Garden Club. Over her drawing-room mantel hung the Gainsborough portrait of the Duchess of Devonshire that her father had bought for a record sum.

narrative_blocks.header: She led wartime relief from a Manhattan mansion
narrative_blocks.body: During the First World War, Louisa Satterlee organized Red Cross and relief work from the family's East Thirty-sixth Street home beside her father's famous library. The Morgan compound became a hub of patrician philanthropy.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Her London estate in Roehampton, formerly connected to the court of Napoleon III.

bio.display_name: Doctor Mabel Morgan Satterlee Satterlee Ingalls
bio.title: Doctor
